name: Cohort Graduation Rate
pillarName: Prepare Each Student for Their Next Phase in Life
menuTooltip: How many of our students graduate from high school?
goal: Increase the adjusted four-year cohort high school graduation rate to 92% by 2030.
definition: The cohort graduation rate measures the percentage of 9th-graders who go on to graduate from high school four years later.
metricText: Percentage of 9th-graders graduating from high school four years later
sourceLabel: NC Department of Public Instruction Cohort Graduation Rate Dashboard
badgeAltText: Highest 4-Year Graduation Rate in NC History
currentDescription: Percentage of 9th-graders graduating from high school four years later (2024–25)
whyItCounts: Graduation rate is the most widely recognized measure of a public school system’s success. Every student who earns a diploma gains access to more career opportunities, higher lifetime earnings, and better health outcomes.

whatsInMotion.title: Finish Line Grants
whatsInMotion.description: Districts receive targeted funding to support students who need interventions — tutoring, transportation, mentoring — to cross the graduation finish line.

whatsInMotion.title: Early Warning System
whatsInMotion.description: Schools use data-driven indicators to identify students at risk of not graduating and connect them with support services early.

nextUpdate: Data for the next cohort, those in 9th-grade in 2022-23, will be available in September, 2026.

name: ACT Composite Score
pillarName: Prepare Each Student for Their Next Phase in Life
menuTooltip: How do our 11th-graders score on the national ACT assessment?
goal: Increase the average ACT composite score to 20 by 2030.
definition: The ACT is a national college-entrance test. This measure is North Carolina's average composite score (out of 36) for each graduating class. In North Carolina, all 11th graders take the ACT each spring as part of the state's school accountability system.
whyItCounts: Every North Carolina junior takes the ACT, giving families and educators a common measure of college readiness. Students who score higher open doors to more college choices and stronger career opportunities.

whatsInMotion.title: ACT Prep Course
whatsInMotion.description: High school students take a dedicated preparation course before the ACT, building the skills and confidence they need to score higher.

whatsInMotion.title: Instructional Coaching
whatsInMotion.description: Instructional coaches work alongside teachers to strengthen daily lessons in reading, writing, and math — the foundations students need for the ACT.

name: AP Participation
pillarName: Prepare Each Student for Their Next Phase in Life
menuTooltip: How many of our high school students earn college credit by passing Advanced Placement exams?
goal: Increase the Advanced Placement (AP) participation rate among 10th, 11th and 12th grade students to 29% by 2030.
definition: Advanced Placement (AP) exams are college-level tests in subjects like English or calculus. This measure is the share of 10th-, 11th-, and 12th-graders who take at least one AP exam each year.
metricText: Percentage of 10th, 11th, and 12th-graders who take an Advanced Placement exam
badgeAltText: Highest AP Scores & Participation in NC History
currentDescription: Percentage of 10th, 11th, and 12th-graders who take an Advanced Placement exam (2024–25)
whyItCounts: Taking Advanced Placement courses challenges students with college-level work before they leave high school. Students who take on that challenge build skills and confidence that carry into college and careers.

whatsInMotion.title: Open Doors to AP
whatsInMotion.description: Schools expand access to Advanced Placement, International Baccalaureate, and other rigorous courses so every student can take on a college-level challenge.

whatsInMotion.title: Instructional Coaching
whatsInMotion.description: Coaches help teachers refine their instruction so more students are prepared to take on the challenge of Advanced Placement courses.

name: AP Performance
pillarName: Prepare Each Student for Their Next Phase in Life
menuTooltip: How many of our high school students earn college credit by passing Advanced Placement exams?
goal: Increase the percentage of 10th, 11th and 12th grade students who pass an AP exam with a score of 3, 4, or 5 to 75% by 2030.
definition: Advanced Placement (AP) exams are scored 1 to 5. This measure is the share of 10th-, 11th-, and 12th-graders who score a 3 or higher, the level that usually earns college credit.
metricText: Percentage of 10th, 11th, and 12th-graders who pass an Advanced Placement exam
badgeAltText: Highest AP Scores & Participation in NC History
currentDescription: Percentage of 10th, 11th, and 12th-graders who pass an Advanced Placement exam (2024–25)
whyItCounts: Passing an AP exam proves a student can master college-level material. Students who earn a passing score can receive college credit, saving their families time and tuition while getting a head start on a degree.

whatsInMotion.title: Teaching & Learning Framework
whatsInMotion.description: A shared framework sets clear expectations for high-quality instruction, so teachers can deliver the rigorous lessons that help AP students pass the exam.

whatsInMotion.title: Stronger Teacher Training
whatsInMotion.description: New standards for teacher training ensure educators get the high-quality preparation they need to help students master AP material.

nextUpdate: [Add note on when data next available]

name: Dual Enrollment Participation
pillarName: Prepare Each Student for Their Next Phase in Life
menuTooltip: How many of our high school students earn credit in dual enrollment programs with local colleges?
goal: Increase the percentage of high school graduates that enroll in at least one dual enrollment course to 41% by 2030.
definition: Dual enrollment lets high school students take courses at a local community college or university, earning both high school and college credit. This measure is the share of graduates who enrolled in at least one such course.
metricText: Percentage of high school graduates that enroll in at least one dual enrollment course
currentDescription: Percentage of high school graduates that enroll in at least one dual enrollment course (2024–25)
whyItCounts: Dual enrollment lets students take real college courses while still in high school. Students who participate enroll in college at higher rates and arrive at college with momentum already behind them.

whatsInMotion.title: College Access Programs
whatsInMotion.description: Programs like NC College Connect and NextNC give students a direct path to enroll in college courses while still in high school.

whatsInMotion.title: College & Career Toolkits
whatsInMotion.description: Communities, businesses, and families get toolkits to guide students in planning their path — including enrolling in college courses before graduation.

name: Dual Enrollment Credit
pillarName: Prepare Each Student for Their Next Phase in Life
menuTooltip: How many of our high school students earn credit in dual enrollment programs with local colleges?
goal: Increase the percentage of high school graduates who earn high school credit through dual enrollment courses to 41% by 2030.
definition: Dual enrollment is a program where high school students take college courses. This measure is the share of graduates who earned high school credit this way.
metricText: Percentage of high school graduates who earn high school credit in at least one dual enrollment course
currentDescription: Percentage of high school graduates who earn high school credit in at least one dual enrollment course (2023–24)
whyItCounts: Enrolling in a college course matters, but earning the credit proves a student can do the work. Students who finish with credit shorten their path to a degree and reduce tuition costs for their families.

whatsInMotion.title: Digital Learning Tools
whatsInMotion.description: High-quality online content and personalized digital tools give students the support to succeed in college-level courses and earn the credit.

whatsInMotion.title: Attendance Counts
whatsInMotion.description: Schools share proven strategies to improve attendance — because students who show up are far more likely to earn their credit.

name: CTE Participation
pillarName: Prepare Each Student for Their Next Phase in Life
menuTooltip: How many of our high school students earn credits in Career and Technical Education programs?
goal: Increase the percentage of all students participating in a CTE course to 41% by 2030.
definition: Career and Technical Education (CTE) courses teach job-ready skills in fields like health care, technology, and the building trades. This measure is the share of K-12 students taking at least one CTE course. Calculated as CTE enrollment from federal Perkins reporting divided by total K-12 enrollment from NCES Common Core of Data.
metricText: Percentage of all students participating in a Career and Technical Education course
currentDescription: Percentage of all students participating in a Career and Technical Education course (2023–24)
whyItCounts: Career and Technical Education connects classroom learning to the workplace. Students who participate in CTE build practical skills and stay engaged in school, preparing them for jobs or further education.

whatsInMotion.title: Career Development Plans
whatsInMotion.description: Students create personalized plans mapping the courses, credentials, and experiences that connect their time in school to their career goals.

whatsInMotion.title: Business Partnerships for Schools
whatsInMotion.description: Businesses and community groups invest in innovative school programs — bringing funding and real-world partnerships that support career learning.

name: CTE Credentials
pillarName: Prepare Each Student for Their Next Phase in Life
menuTooltip: How many of our high school students earn credits in Career and Technical Education programs?
goal: Increase the number of CTE credentials earned by students yearly to 500,000 by 2030.
definition: A CTE credential is an industry certificate students can earn in high school, such as Microsoft Office Specialist or Certified Nurse Aide. This measure is the total number of credentials earned by North Carolina students each year.
metricText: Number of Career and Technical Education credentials earned by students
badgeAltText: Highest CTE Credentials Earned in NC History
currentDescription: Number of Career and Technical Education credentials earned by students (2024–25)
whyItCounts: Millions of good-paying jobs do not require a four-year degree. Students who earn industry credentials can show employers they have real-world skills — and that proof helps them land stronger jobs.

whatsInMotion.title: Rigorous Career Pathways
whatsInMotion.description: Career and technical programs are growing — giving more students the chance to earn the industry credentials that employers value.

whatsInMotion.title: Education Networks
whatsInMotion.description: Education networks connect schools to share what works — spreading the practices that help more students earn the credentials employers want.

name: NAEP Grade 4 Reading
pillarName: Prepare Each Student for Their Next Phase in Life
menuTooltip: How do our 4th- and 8th-graders score in reading on the National Assessment of Educational Progress?
goal: Increase the average scale score on the 4th grade reading NAEP assessment to 225 by 2030.
definition: NAEP (the Nation's Report Card) is given every two years to a student sample in all 50 states. Reading scores run 0 to 500; a 208 counts as Basic and 238 as Proficient. This measure is North Carolina's average 4th-grade score.
metricText: Average scale score on 4th grade reading national assessment (NAEP)
currentDescription: Average scale score on 4th grade reading national assessment (NAEP) (2024)
whyItCounts: Students who read well by fourth grade are far more likely to graduate. This is the moment when children shift from learning to read to reading to learn every other subject.

whatsInMotion.title: Science of Reading
whatsInMotion.description: Schools adopt research-based reading instruction, giving children the strongest foundation for learning to read by fourth grade.

whatsInMotion.title: 10 Million Books
whatsInMotion.description: A statewide campaign puts 10 million books in students’ hands each year — growing a love of reading that powers learning.

name: NAEP Grade 8 Reading
pillarName: Prepare Each Student for Their Next Phase in Life
menuTooltip: How do our 4th- and 8th-graders score in reading on the National Assessment of Educational Progress?
goal: Increase the average scale score on the 8th grade reading NAEP assessment to 268 by 2030.
definition: NAEP (the Nation's Report Card) is given every two years to a student sample in all 50 states. Reading scores run 0 to 500; a 243 counts as Basic and 281 as Proficient. This measure is North Carolina's average 8th-grade score.
metricText: Average scale score on 8th grade reading national assessment (NAEP)
currentDescription: Average scale score on 8th grade reading national assessment (NAEP) (2024)
whyItCounts: By eighth grade, reading is the tool students use to learn science, history, and every other subject. Strong readers enter high school ready to handle rigorous coursework across all classes.

whatsInMotion.title: Science of Reading
whatsInMotion.description: Research-based reading practices extend through middle school, so eighth-graders build the comprehension skills they need for every subject.

whatsInMotion.title: Focused Learning Time
whatsInMotion.description: Districts adopt best practices to limit wireless device distractions, giving students more focused time to read, learn, and think deeply.

name: NAEP Grade 4 Math
pillarName: Prepare Each Student for Their Next Phase in Life
menuTooltip: How do our 4th- and 8th-graders score in math on the National Assessment of Educational Progress?
goal: Increase the average scale score on the 4th grade math NAEP assessment to 246 by 2030.
definition: NAEP (the Nation's Report Card) is given every two years to a student sample in all 50 states. Math scores run 0 to 500; a 214 counts as Basic and 249 as Proficient. This measure is North Carolina's average 4th-grade score.
metricText: Average scale score on 4th grade mathematics national assessment (NAEP)
currentDescription: Average scale score on 4th grade mathematics national assessment (NAEP) (2024)
whyItCounts: When children build strong math skills early, the advantage stays with them for years. Students who master the basics — like fractions, patterns, and problem-solving — carry that confidence through every grade that follows.

whatsInMotion.title: Golden LEAF Schools
whatsInMotion.description: The Golden LEAF Schools Initiative brings high-quality math instruction to rural schools, helping children develop strong early math skills.

whatsInMotion.title: Family Learning Academy
whatsInMotion.description: A virtual academy gives families short, accessible online sessions on how to support their child’s learning at home — including early math.

name: NAEP Grade 8 Math
pillarName: Prepare Each Student for Their Next Phase in Life
menuTooltip: How do our 4th- and 8th-graders score in math on the National Assessment of Educational Progress?
goal: Increase the average scale score on the 8th grade math NAEP assessment to 283 by 2030.
definition: NAEP (the Nation's Report Card) is given every two years to a student sample in all 50 states. Math scores run 0 to 500; a 262 counts as Basic and 299 as Proficient. This measure is North Carolina's average 8th-grade score.
metricText: Average scale score on 8th grade mathematics national assessment (NAEP)
currentDescription: Average scale score on 8th grade mathematics national assessment (NAEP) (2024)
whyItCounts: Eighth-grade math determines whether students can access algebra, calculus, and other advanced courses. Students who master these skills keep the door open to college and STEM career opportunities.

whatsInMotion.title: Golden LEAF Schools
whatsInMotion.description: The Golden LEAF Schools Initiative transforms math instruction in rural schools through a proven model, raising expectations and results.

whatsInMotion.title: Better Assessments, Better Teaching
whatsInMotion.description: New progress checks give teachers timely data on what students know — so they can adjust math instruction before it’s too late.

name: Schools of Character
pillarName: Prepare Each Student for Their Next Phase in Life
goal: Increase the number of North Carolina public schools recognized as National Schools of Character by Character.org (now CharacterPlus).
definition: Under development — the definition and data source for this measure are still being finalized.
metricText: Number of schools recognized as National Schools of Character
currentDescription: Number of schools recognized as National Schools of Character
whyItCounts: Schools that teach character alongside academics help students do better in class and in life. Students in these schools build habits like teamwork, resilience, and responsibility that support success beyond the classroom.

whatsInMotion.title: Character Education Toolkit
whatsInMotion.description: A statewide toolkit helps schools teach values like respect, responsibility, and perseverance — the character traits that shape good citizens.

whatsInMotion.title: Whole School, Whole Child
whatsInMotion.description: Schools create supportive environments where every student feels they belong — fostering the positive climate where strong character grows.

name: Public School Enrollment
pillarName: Galvanize Champions to Fully Invest in and Support Public Education
menuTooltip: How many school-aged children are enrolled in NC public schools?
goal: Increase the percentage of school-aged children enrolled in public schools to 89% by 2030.
definition: This measure is the share of North Carolina's school-aged children enrolled in public schools (traditional, charter, lab, or regional public schools).
metricText: Percentage of school-aged children enrolled in public schools
sourceLabel: Calculated by the NC Department of Public Instruction. Public school enrollment from Table 1 and Table 3 of the NCDPI Statistical Profile. Private school enrollment from the NC Statistical Summary for Private Schools (NC Department of Administration). Home school enrollment from the NC Statistical Summary for Home Schools (NC Department of Administration).
currentDescription: Percentage of school-aged children enrolled in public schools (2024–25)
whyItCounts: Public schools serve every child regardless of income, ability, or background — providing meals, special education, and essential support services. Research shows students in diverse schools do better academically and build stronger connections.

whatsInMotion.title: Sharing Success Stories
whatsInMotion.description: Educators and community partners share stories of student achievement and school success — showing families the value of their public schools.

whatsInMotion.title: Our Public Schools
whatsInMotion.description: Communities across North Carolina share how public education shapes their neighborhoods and prepares children for the future.
